title: Masala Chai Recipe: Authentic Indian Tea, Step by Step
meta_description: Make authentic Indian masala chai at home with whole spices, black tea, and fresh milk. Step-by-step ratios, timings, and answers to the most common questions.
body_html:
Masala chai is black tea simmered with whole spices and milk — not the syrupy concentrate found in most coffee shops. Real chai is closer to a soup than a steeped tea: the spices are bloomed in liquid, the tea steeps in the same pot, and the entire drink is finished with milk and sugar before straining.

This recipe makes two cups in about 12 minutes.

Ingredients

  • 1½ cups water
  • 1½ cups whole milk (or oat milk)
  • 2 teaspoons loose-leaf black tea (Assam is traditional — CTC if you want strength, orthodox for nuance)
  • 4 green cardamom pods, gently cracked
  • 1 cinnamon stick (about 1 inch)
  • 4 whole cloves
  • 4 whole black peppercorns
  • 1 thin slice fresh ginger (about ¼ inch thick)
  • 1–2 teaspoons sugar or jaggery, to taste

Method

  1. Bloom the spices. Combine water, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, peppercorns, and ginger in a small saucepan. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 4 minutes. The water should turn faintly amber and smell like baked apples and clove.
  2. Add the tea. Drop in the loose tea. Simmer 2 minutes. The liquid will turn deep mahogany.
  3. Add milk and sugar. Pour in the milk and stir in the sugar. Raise heat until the chai reaches a rolling foam — right when it threatens to boil over, pull it off the heat. Repeat this raise-and-pull motion two or three times. This is what builds chai's signature creamy body.
  4. Strain and serve. Pour through a fine-mesh strainer into pre-warmed cups. Drink immediately.

The single most common mistake

Adding the tea at the same time as the milk. Tea needs to steep in water first — milk fat blocks the extraction of tannins, leaving the cup pale and flat. Always: water + spices, then tea, then milk.

Variations worth trying

Strong masala chai (north Indian street style)

Double the tea, add 1 star anise to the spice mix, and let the milk reduce visibly before pulling. The cup should coat a spoon.

Soft masala chai (south Indian style)

Halve the cardamom, skip pepper and clove, increase ginger. Cleaner, more floral.

Vegan masala chai

Oat milk is the closest substitute for whole milk — coconut milk overpowers the spices. Use a barista-style oat milk that doesn't split when boiled.

Storage

Drink masala chai fresh. The tannins in steeped black tea oxidize within an hour and the flavor turns flat. If you want chai ready on demand, see our chai concentrate recipe — the concentrate keeps in the fridge for two weeks and only needs hot milk to serve.

For more on the differences between traditional masala chai, modern chai latte, and other chai-style drinks, see our guide on what is a chai latte.

faqs.q: Do I have to use whole spices?
faqs.a: Yes, if you want the real flavor. Pre-ground masala chai mixes oxidize quickly and taste muddy. Whole spices bloom in hot water and release oils gradually — that's what makes the drink layered.

faqs.q: What black tea is best for masala chai?
faqs.a: Assam CTC (crush-tear-curl) is the traditional choice and what every chai stall in India uses. It brews strong and stands up to milk. Orthodox Assam, Nilgiri, or even Ceylon black tea also work and give more nuance.

faqs.q: Can I use a tea bag?
faqs.a: Tea bags work in a pinch but most contain dust-grade tea that brews bitter. Loose leaf gives you 3× better flavor for the same caffeine.

faqs.q: Why does my chai taste weak?
faqs.a: Three usual causes: too much milk relative to water (use 1:1, not more milk), tea added with milk instead of water-first, or under-simmered spices. The bloom step (water + spices, 4 minutes) is what builds depth.

The unsweetened version is genuinely good for you: black tea polyphenols, ginger and cardamom anti-inflammatories, cinnamon for blood sugar. Heavy sugar undermines the benefit — aim for 1 teaspoon per cup or less.
